item.title: Re: SCP-077 Runes
item.content:encoded:
I like the suggestions! The hard part is taking these words and making it like poetic.

I was thinking, and I don't got my mind set on this but… maybe having it be a full passage like a longer poem or ritual saying and having it change to a different line each Lunar month.

It could also be like a passage that relates to that month in the culture and traditions of the Druids of Ireland.

item.title: Re: SCP-077 Runes
item.content:encoded:
If you're saying that you have the ability to take a properly-licensed image of the top half of a human skull from Wikimedia Commons and photoshop an appropriate set of Ogham runes onto it, so that it can be used in the article… that would be a wonderful idea.

Let's see, it would definitely need to include the words "HUNGER" and "PROTECTION"… and "PAYMENT". And probably "GRATITUDE". And "CHANGE". And "ROT". And maybe "LOYALTY" or "DUTY" or "HONOR" — or "BETRAYAL".

And at least one insult.

And something that's obviously an idiom, but whose meaning is unclear. Maybe this is the section that changes by itself?

item.title: SCP-077 Runes
item.content:encoded:
Howdy,

So something that really bugs me when trying to make content with SCP-077 such as roleplays and whatnot is that we have no clue what the Runes exactly are. I would like to possibly create some runes for it, if not an official edit then at least for my own private activities.

The thing is though while I have the ability to translate English to Irish to Ogham, I'm not to sure what it should exactly say per lunar passage. Does anyone have any suggestions or know of anything that may already exist in that language which would be helpful?

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: Incidentally
item.content:encoded:
That list of side effects?

nausea, cramps, headache, dizziness, incontinence, fever, skin rashes, nosebleeds, and fugue states

those are symptoms of solanine poisoning.

which you get from eating green potatoes.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - Wikiwalk
item.content:encoded: The REDACTED link goes to SCP-1869 and not SCP-1689.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - Wikiwalk
item.content:encoded: Took me a while to figure out the right connection, but I did eventually get it.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - Wikiwalk
item.content:encoded: I'm suggesting an inline crosslink in the paragraph I quoted, on the words "potato tubers", as present in the proposal. This could be a footnote or added line, saying something like "Research of a connection between SCP-077 and SCP-1689 could also work.", if you would prefer that.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - Wikiwalk
item.content:encoded: Be more specific. What do you intend.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Wikiwalk
item.content:encoded:
Edits made; timer removed.

The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

The most easy avenue for a crosslink here would be another potato based SCP, of which there are a few. One of these is SCP-1689, for which there are thematic similarities both in the potato-based anomaly and the nature of the anomaly's discovery.

Therefore, I propose a crosslink here:

All biological material (with the obvious exception of SCP-077 itself) which comes in contact with SCP-077-1 is instantly transformed into a viscous, malodorous ooze; the ooze has been identified as the rotted flesh of potato tubers (Solanum tuberosum) which have been severely infected with the potato blight (Phytophthora infestans). One (1) cubic centimeter of SCP-077-1 transforms upwards of eight hundred (800) grams of biological material.

If anyone has any other ideas, feel free to suggest them below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HAHAHAHAHAHAHAHAHAHAHA!

Potatoes…

+1'ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd somehow missed that on my first read, so thanks for pointing it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No. If you wait too long to read the runes, there's an effect that builds up, and it hits you pretty damn hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … the cafeteria excludes potatoes and potato-based items because rune-readers have an elevated risk of developing lethal potato allergies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but the one thing that keeps bugging me is this:

The cafeteria menu for Research Sector-861 must not include any potatoes or potato-based ingredients.

Why? There is no reason given, nor is there any explanation as to what happens if this isn't followed. (Also, since the majority of packaged foods contain potato starch in some measure, this requirement is almost impossible to implement unless the site has it's own farm to supply the cafeteria.)

On a side note, a possibility that it does what it does if the runes aren't read popped into my head. The skull is, itself, a "containment procedure"…it would contain the blight, keeping it from the village. Once the blight passed, however, it had nothing to "feed" off of thus making it go bad, explaining why it goes from being referred to as a "protector" to being referred to in darker terms. Another thought I had was that the side effects of reading the runes are because while the readers may "understand" them, they don't understand the "deeper meanings" of them as someone who was actually a druid would, thus they get a bit of "backlash" because while they know the meaning and pronunciation, they aren't in the proper mindset of "understanding" that it requires to properly use it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this article, but something's bothering me:

Effects intensify as the time between readings increases, and can become cumulative for individuals who read the engravings too many times consecutively and/or too frequently. Readers have a 60% chance of developing an allergy to potatoes.

Shouldn't it be "decreases" to mean that the effects get worse as readings become more frequent?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Suggested reading I've always headcanoned as somehow related:
More potatoes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… potatoes.

I'm not creeped out by much, but the idea that this thing was beneficial and then got screwed up (perhaps because it felt it wasn't appreciated; not that it's sentient, but you know how magic can be) is facinating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be required reading for how to write interesting Containment Procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the few SCPs to bother me and I think it's because my diet consists of like eighty percent potato.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate to be nitpicky with such a generally solid and original SCP, but simple science errors tend to undermine my suspension of disbelief.

One (1) cubic centimeter of SCP-077-1 transforms upwards of eight hundred (800) grams of biological material.

Since SCP-077-1 behaves as a gas, its volume is variable based on pressure (or available space, depending on how you look at it). Giving a volume without noting pressure is meaningless. Alternately, you could record it by mass, but that might be odd.

Not being a scientist myself, I might be probably am wrong, but it seems the best format might be "X g of SCP-077-1 (occupying one (1) cubic centimeter at 0 °C and 1.01x10^5pa) transforms upwards of eight hundred (800) grams of biological material."
Again, good article, but with a sciencey nitpick.

item.title: Re: Proposal
item.content:encoded: Then why didn't you warn us BEFORE we read the SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An acursed Irish skull that turns everything into rotten potatoes? The only way to make this more Irish would be to include copious amounts of booze
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't fuck with potatoes, son.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. this is one of my favorite containment procedures on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh… wha… uh… so it's a skull that makes things into potatoes?

Like me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Right. The best way to summarize the carvings would be "runic script", as many of the carvings seem to span several of the runic alphabets. The presence of the triquetra, while not a rune per say, but still having presence among Germanic runestones, supports this. However, they don't seem centralized to one specific runic language.

However, there are a few symbols with origins that are questionably not runic, but still related. Two symbols I identified may actually be Ogham.

Anyways, like I said, it's best to just state they're "runic script". If necessary, I can detail the possibilities of what each symbol may be, to let you have more say.

item.title: Regarding the markings
item.content:encoded: I'm on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As for your first point: so what? We don't know how it came to be protecting the village from the Famine. It could have been centuries old when it began protecting the village, or maybe the villagers gave it ancient runes for some reason.

As for your second point: oops. How would you describe those markings, then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Problem: potatoes weren't introduced to Ireland until approximately 900 years after people stopped using Ogham. Furthermore, the etchings on the skull in the picture don't look a thing like Ogham writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'know, it's probably a sign of my sad state of mind that the first thing I thought of was "BECAUSE I'M A POTATO." Nevertheless, I think this one is just weird and quirky enough to warrant an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like almost everything about this one except the potato theme. I know it is pivotal in many ways to the story plot, but it seems too cliche. If I submitted a SCP about Florida, must it involve oranges? I won't downvote, it is well written and at least it isn't about St. Patrick who was a leprechaun who ate shamrocks. I do like it, but… potato.

PS I really love the picture, sets the mood nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. It made me giggle and go WTF at the same time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, good rewrite. Vote changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvote switched to upvote. Instead of annoying, this is now interesting, original, and intriguing. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like the new version enough for an upvote, but it's definitely an improvement.

The cafeteria menu for Research Sector-██ may not include any potatoes or potato-based ingredients.

You see the image of the skull, read about runes and steel-reinforced walls, then come to that and think "what the heck?". Heheheh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I approve of the edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Duly, and gleefully, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the ooze has been identified as the rotted flesh of potato tubers (Solanum tuberosum) which have been severely infected with the potato blight (Phytophthora infestans).

Upon reading the above conversation, and this bit of the rewrite…

Upvoted. Yes.

item.title: Re: Proposal
item.content:encoded: Readers have a 60% chance of developing an allergy to potatoes.

item.title: Re: Proposal
item.content:encoded: Also in favour of this rewrite.

item.title: Re: Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Suggestions/ideas for potato-related effects:

  1. Anyone who reads the runes (and/or touches the SCP, sees it, or gets too close to it) is permanently altered so that eating potatoes is a requirement. If they don't eat potatoes every few days, or if potatoes don't constitute a large enough percentage of their diet, then they start starving, no matter how much non-potato food they eat.
  2. In conjunction with #1, any potatoes (or maybe any vegetables whatsoever) grown within _ km of the SCP are bitter tasting and slightly stunted.
  3. Alternatively to #2, any potatoes or potato products that pass within _ km of the SCP become bitter tasting and lose some of their nutritional value. The radius of the effect is growing, which forced the SCP to be moved from the first Foundation facility it was housed at, since the facility was close enough to a fast-food place that all their french-fries started tasting awful.

item.title: Re: Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Sounds like a cool idea.

And when the Famine was over… well, they didn't need it any more, and they tried to get rid of it. But something went horribly wrong, and generations of villagers spent their whole lives doing their best to keep it under control.

Hmmm, if the thing has no permanent ill effects when there's multiple people who share doing the reading, it doesn't seem like keeping it under control would be that big a deal. If the village has 30 literate people, then any one of them will only get nauseous and feverish about once a month if it's read exactly once every 24 hours, or mildly nauseous and mildly feverish about three times a month if it's read once every eight hours. And having to contain the thing would give them a great incentive to make sure everyone is literate, so the work could be shared among as many people as possible.

A suggestion for coming up with what it costs to contain it: while the villagers were actually getting use out of it during the Great Famine, they had to periodically do something to keep it satisfied. After the tried to get rid of it, the cost of containing it became an intensified and corrupted version of what they were doing before.

Also, it might be interesting if potatoes were somehow involved in its containment. For instance, if it was a requirement that the people reading runes had to be growing potatoes, or the rune reading doesn't work.

item.title: Re: Proposal
item.content:encoded: If you write this (well), I will gleefully upvote it.

item.title: Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Okay, to start with, let's get rid of the guaranteed mortality rate. Reading the runes can make you nauseous, it can screw with your blood pressure, it can give you a fever… but it won't kill you. Unless you're the only one reading the runes and you do it too many times in a row. The runes have to be read at least once every 24 hours Or Else, but if we do it every 8 hours, the health effects are more manageable.

(We could do them every 6 hours, but at that point, the strain of how often they have to do the readings outweighs the strain of letting the effect build up for a full day.)

Second: the Cloud of Doom which emerges from 077 if we don't read the runes on cue.

What it does is it turns biological material into rotten potato flesh. Specifically, potato flesh that's contaminated with Phytophthora infestans, the potato blight.

In the mid-19th century, the villagers somehow made this thing, or summoned it, or whatever, to protect them from the Gorta Mór, the Great Hunger.

And it worked. For 7 years, it protected/that village — and only that village — from the Blight and the resulting Drochshaol (Bad Times).

And when the Famine was over… well, they didn't need it any more, and they tried to get rid of it. But something went horribly wrong, and generations of villagers spent their whole lives doing their best to keep it under control.

Hm?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the druidic stuff, and I'm actually okay with the 5% rate (that just sounds like a good number). In my opinion, the thing to do is drop the frequency of readings necessary from once every day to once every few days or once a week. Something to provide a little more breathing room if things go wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's if every single component of containment goes according to schedule. If the translation on the runes gets held up by anything (I've never translated runes before, so what the hell do I know), that's a 24-hour time bomb until [REDACTED HORROR] happens, which will [REDACTED VERB] [REDACTED NUMBER OF PEOPLE]. Besides, mathematically, this means every D-class trained in this will likely die well ahead of schedule, and the Foundation must have linguists on hand both for the training and for the translation, and this assumes that all/most of the D-class recruited are capable of learning this sort of thing that quickly (not everyone can learn a skill this precise to this level of quality this quickly). Some component of this has to fail somewhat often, and this file doesn't even tell you what happens when it *does* fail.

While we're at it, I wouldn't mind an addendum with a few samples of translated runes, just for atmosphere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One day of training doesn't really make them specially trained, and at with "only" a 5% chance of death every 12 hours, that gives the average D-Class a halflife of something like a week (I think).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One detail I really do want to keep is that this relies on lunar months and eclipses. How about if we drop the fatality rate for readers, and give D-class an extension for as long as they're useful here?

De-redacting to come over the weekend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The containment procedures could use revision. Right now, whether it's stated clearly or not, this is burning through a hell of a lot of D-class personnel, all of whom have to be specially trained. I'd reduce the frequency of how often the runes have to be read. That, along with everything you said, plus a revisiting of the second addendum to compensate for altering 077-1 (and somebody's going to have to deredact *something* about what this thing actually does, period, and preferably also what it does to human tissue).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's going downhill. Any suggestions on how we might improve this thing?

(I've got standing permission from Gabriel Jade to tinker with this thing as necessary.)

How about clarifying somewhat the effect of 077-1 , and also being more specific re: the connection to druidism? Would that help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this for no good reason whatsoever. I have downvoted SCPs in the past for having problems similar to this, but this I just like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept is okay but there is no real information there as to what it is, or does, or why the excessive containment measures are there. It is basically like saying, "here is this huge difficult procedure you have to do or else… some stuff happens. Look, just do it because I said so!" I mean there is no information beyond that it has to be contained and read. Everything else is expunged. It really makes the whole thing seem like a pointless waste of time and resources.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure I like having a sub-SCP (077-1) whose nature and effects are completely redacted. To me, that feels kind of like a cop-out. "We won't tell you what this is, but it's totally scary and dangerous, honest." When redacting major facts/features, it helps to include enough non-redacted information to provide at least some prompting. For all we know like this, 077-1 could be particularly low-quality orange marmalade, and the Foundation just hates cleaning it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did some minor tweaking with this one, tried to get rid of the refrigerator logic as well as tweaking some of the numbers. I seem to have forgotten the beauty of ████ text. Still not sure about the mortality rate from simply reading the thing, but I'll let it stand as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
so this is an object that if a disaster happened and personnel onsite were all killed so that it was uncontained for a couple days right at the time the runes changed, would then proceed to destroy the world because nobody could get to it to find out what the new runes were? one that requires constant attention and frequent loss of D-class personnel to keep it in line?

freaky as a concept but i have to ask why the hell something like this is Euclid. it's not easily contained, that's a freakin lot of containment procedures you have to have in place, one day's inattention is enough to kill everyone onsite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing we could do is say that the disintegration effect only works at ground level. That'd also help with why it doesn't affect liquids.

The effect is in a circle, not a sphere.

If you're in the top floors of a building and the bottom floors get disintegrated, you have a chance of surviving (until the building collapses WTC-style, of course).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know, I just got hit with some fridge logic here upon reading this.

When the Foundation captured it, it started its disintegration effect, eventually going to a radius of seven kilometers. So how in the hell could anyone actually get to the damned thing to read the runes and get it to stop?

The Earth's crust in Ireland is over 20 km deep, so we won't be hitting lava, thank God. However, we're talking about the disintegration of over 260 cubic kilometers of Earth, or about 390 gigatonnes. Where did it go? Is it truly disintegrated, or did it become dust and get blown away, or what, exactly?

If a meteorite made a crater that big in Ireland, it would probably have hit with a force equal to 20 gigatonnes of TNT. Ireland would have been sterilized, and we might have a tsunami in the Atlantic, along with global cooling and other effects. So how does that explanation hold water? (Aside: The Chicxulub crater, the dinosaur killer? That crater was about ten kilometers wide.)

I like the concept, although I'm going to have to downvote the article, I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hum, I liked the mental image the mist gave me. It made this feel more like a old horror movie, or that the skull was actually a prison for the mist, or any number of things that doesn't affect the containment procedures so the Foundation doesn't really care about. On the otherhand, I might be in the minority with my preference here. So I say just make it as you like it, and if you want to stay with the mist, just say it absorbs all kinetic energy of whatever it disintegrates, to avoid the argument of using circulating air to trap it.

Otherwise, better. Gets my vote now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I like the idea that the reason there is a mortality rate at all is because we're doing it wrong somehow. Thats how I justified it to myself, at least.
